coredata.dc:title: Modulating the properties of graphene quantum dots by heteroatom doping for biomedical applications
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Kansara, Vrushti
Patel, Mitali
coredata.dc:description: The optical properties of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) can be modulated by heteroatom doping. Different type of heteroatom with discrete electron clouds determine their suitability for various biomedical applications. With this consideration, this research work emphasizes on the influence of different type of heteroatom doping (N, S and B) on the properties of GQDs. The change in the structure, chemical composition and electronic transition was characterized using different analytical techniques. The hemolytic activity, free radical scavenging activity, colloidal stability and surface morphology were also determined. The N doped GQDs showed compact structure (3.32 ± 1.38 nm), increased sp2 hybridization (ID/IG ratio: 0.12) and eventually higher QY value (80.31 ± 2.56 %) than the S and B doped GQDs. The doped GQDs were found to be hemocompatible (<5 %). The N-GQDs exhibited high free radical scavenging activity (94.51 ± 2.05 %). The doped GQDs showed good colloidal stability in the presence of bovine serum albumin (BSA), different salt concentration and pH. The N-GQDs retained their fluorescence property at temperature up to 80 °C and displayed no damage to the α-helical structure of BSA. The N-GQDs exhibited uniformly distributed spherical particles in the range of 1–5 nm. The selected area diffraction (SAED) pattern represented a well-arranged hexagonal crystalline structure of the N-GQDs which could provide long-term fluorescence stability. Overall, the study proved the N-GQDs as appropriate nanomaterial for various biomedical applications as compared to the S-GQDs and B-GQDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Heteroatom doping
sp2 hybridization
Electronic transitions
SAED pattern

Electronic transitions of the doped GQDs
Surface morphology using high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM)
Particle size and zeta potential measurement
Electronic transitions of the doped GQDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We are grateful for the financial support provided under SHODH (Scheme of Developing High Quality Research) scheme to Vrushti Kansara by the Education Department, Government of Gujarat. We also offer our sincere gratitude to IIT Bombay and IIT Sonipat for providing central laboratory facilities for analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic representation of hydrothermally prepared N-GQDs (A), S-GQDs (B) and B-GQDs (C).
The overlay of UV–visible spectra of the doped GQDs.
The emission spectra of the doped GQDs acquired at λex of 365 nm (The images on the left side of the spectra are captured under UV irradiation of 366 nm).
The XPS fitting curves of the doped GQDs showing N1s (A), S2p (B), B1s (C), C1s (D, E, F) and O1s (G, H, I) peaks.
The fluorescence intensity (A) and zeta potential (B) of the doped GQDs at different doped GQDs:BSA ratio.
The fluorescence intensity (A) and zeta potential (B) of the doped GQDs at different NaCl concentration.
The fluorescence intensity (A) and zeta potential (B) of the doped GQDs at different pH.
The CD spectra showing effect of the doped GQDs on the structure of BSA.

The HR-TEM image of the N-GQDs magnified at a scale of 20 nm (A) with particle size distribution (B), The lattice pattern of the N-GQDs (scale bar: 2 nm) (C) and the SAED pattern of the N-GQDs with inset showing d spacing value (D).
The properties of the doped GQDs.

head.ce:title: Modulating the properties of graphene quantum dots by heteroatom doping for biomedical applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Maliba Pharmacy College, Uka Tarsadia University, Surat 394350, Gujarat, India.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Maliba Pharmacy College, Uka Tarsadia University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Maliba Pharmacy College, Uka Tarsadia University, Gujarat, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The optical properties of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) can be modulated by heteroatom doping. Different type of heteroatom with discrete electron clouds determine their suitability for various biomedical applications. With this consideration, this research work emphasizes on the influence of different type of heteroatom doping (N, S and B) on the properties of GQDs. The change in the structure, chemical composition and electronic transition was characterized using different analytical techniques. The hemolytic activity, free radical scavenging activity, colloidal stability and surface morphology were also determined. The N doped GQDs showed compact structure (3.32 ± 1.38 nm), increased sp2 hybridization (ID/IG ratio: 0.12) and eventually higher QY value (80.31 ± 2.56 %) than the S and B doped GQDs. The doped GQDs were found to be hemocompatible (<5 %). The N-GQDs exhibited high free radical scavenging activity (94.51 ± 2.05 %). The doped GQDs showed good colloidal stability in the presence of bovine serum albumin (BSA), different salt concentration and pH. The N-GQDs retained their fluorescence property at temperature up to 80 °C and displayed no damage to the α-helical structure of BSA. The N-GQDs exhibited uniformly distributed spherical particles in the range of 1–5 nm. The selected area diffraction (SAED) pattern represented a well-arranged hexagonal crystalline structure of the N-GQDs which could provide long-term fluorescence stability. Overall, the study proved the N-GQDs as appropriate nanomaterial for various biomedical applications as compared to the S-GQDs and B-GQDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have advantageous properties over commercial organic dyes [1]. Organic dyes are commonly used for fluorescence based bioimaging owing to low cost and easy accessibility [2]. However, these dyes can induce toxicity and carcinogenicity [3]. They also have poor fluorescence stability and low resistance to photobleaching which restrict their use in biomedical applications [4]. The unique tunable optical properties, long-term fluorescence stability and high resistance to photobleaching make GQDs a better alternative to commercial organic dyes [5].

The optical properties of GQDs are majorly influenced by process parameters which are shown in our previous research work [6]. Apart from process parameters, heteroatom doping is one of the factors that can alter the optical properties which includes quantum yield (QY) of GQDs for various biomedical applications [7]. The QY is a key factor that determines the suitability of any fluorescent material for bioimaging and biosensing [8]. The QY is a measure of the efficiency of fluorescent material in converting excited photons into emitted electrons [9]. Materials with high QY have bright fluorescence and are resistant to photobleaching. They are also safe for in vivo applications and can be used in relatively small amount which ultimately save time and money as compared to organic dyes [10]. It displays their superiority for long-term fluorescence-based bioimaging, biosensing, and drug delivery monitoring [11]. Despite having good fluorescence property, GQDs showed insufficient long-term fluorescence stability, low QY and low sensitivity [12]. Therefore, the QY of GQDs is often improved through heteroatom doping using various heteroatoms: nitrogen (N), sulfur (S), oxygen (O), boron (B), phosphorus (P), and fluoride (F) [13]. The reports described the importance of heteroatom doping in enhancing fluorescence property of GQDs [14]. Bian and co-workers showed 80 % QY value with the N-doped GQDs to improve the performance of solar devices [15]. Depending on the type and amount of heteroatom doping, the QY value varies [16]. For example, N and B atoms with distinct electronegativity altered the electron density of GQDs and showed different QY values [17]. N atom with electronegativity (3.04) higher than the C atom (2.55) donates electrons via forming bonds with 4 neighbouring C atoms and increases electron density whereas B atom possesses high number of electron vacancies or holes due to lack of electrons. With S doping, the addition of energy level between π to π* transition could enhance fluorescence property of GQDs, besides having electronegativity similar to C (2.58) [18]. P atoms have high affinity towards oxygen, producing defect sites and eventually show QY value lesser than N doping [19]. Dual or co-doping increased optical properties of GQDs via combining density of electron and hole and increased the potential of GQDs for bioimaging and biosensing [20]. In addition to QY value, other properties of GQDs are also affected. The ability to control the amount of reactive oxygen species (ROS) is reported to be increased post doping which is considered beneficial for prevention of metabolic diseases [21]. N doping can contribute to increased photothermal conversion efficiency [22] which is beneficial for enhancement of cancer cell killing by photothermal therapy [23]. The photothermal application with hemo-compatibility allows the utilization of these nanomaterials in other novel applications including laser tissue welding [24]. These nanomaterials can also be beneficial for surgery and implant markers [25]. Moreover, the N-GQDs were able to amplify surface enhanced Raman signal (SERS) over undoped GQDs which can be further used for fast and hassle-free diagnosis [26]. This shows that the properties of GQDs for desirable application can be changed by changing chemistry of GQDs which eventually depends on the nature of heteroatom doping on GQDs. It also depicts the importance of selecting a suitable heteroatom for further utilization in biomedical applications.

Therefore, our research focuses on understanding the effect of different type of heteroatom doping on the properties of GQDs for biomedical applications. The N, S and B doped GQDs were prepared by hydrothermal treatment and examined for the change in properties using different analytical techniques.

Citric acid, urea, bovine serum albumin (BSA) and sodium chloride (NaCl) were purchased from Loba Chemie, India. Sulfur powder was purchased from Labnox, India. Boric acid was procured from S.D. Fine Chemicals, India. Triton-X 100 and 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) were purchased from Dual Life Sciences, India. Deionized water was used as a solvent and was procured from Labdhi Enterprises, India. The DPPH was of analytical grade with 95 % purity. The other chemicals were of analytical grade with 99 % purity.

The doped GQDs were prepared using hydrothermal treatment. For the preparation of the N-GQDs, citric acid and urea were mixed at 1.16:1 ratio with deionized water and poured into a teflon-lined autoclave (25 ml) (Techinstro, India). The solution mixture was then heated at 170 °C for 4 h. The S-GQDs were prepared by heating citric acid and sulfur powder (1.61:1) at 180 °C for 5 h. The B-GQDs were prepared from citric acid and boric acid (2:1) at 190 °C for 5 h. The solutions obtained after completion of the reaction were centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 25 min (4 °C) and the doped GQDs were collected.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Particle size and zeta potential measurement
ce:section.ce:para: The particle size and zeta potential of the doped GQDs were measured using zetasizer (Nano ZS90, Malvern Instruments, U.K.). He-Ne laser was applied to the samples at a scattering angle of 90°.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electronic transitions of the doped GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The change in electronic transitions with the type of heteroatom doping was examined using a UV–visible spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The samples were analysed in the range of 200–800 nm.

The QY value of the doped GQDs was determined using the emission intensity and absorbance of the doped GQDs and reference standard (0.1 M quinine sulphate in H2SO4). The samples and standard were excited with laser (365 nm) and the emission behaviour was observed using spectrofluorometer (Shimadzu RF-5301 PC, Japan). %QY was calculated using following equation [26]:
(1)
% QY
sample
=
% QY
standard
×
I
sample
I
standard
×
A
standard
A
sample
×
η
sample
2
η
standard
2
where, I
sample and I
standard denote integrated area obtained from emission peak of sample and standard, respectively. A
standard and A
sample represent absorbance of standard and sample, respectively.

The change in graphitization of the doped GQDs was evaluated using Raman spectrometer (STR 300, Airix Corporation, Japan). The samples were exposed to a He-Ne laser (532 nm) for 30 s with a power of 10 mW. The data acquisition was performed in the range of 1000–3500 cm−1.

The sample was allowed to dry at room temperature for 2 h and analysed for composition using X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (AXIS Supra, Shimadzu, Japan). The monochrome Al Kα x-ray radiation of 1486.6 eV was imparted on the samples. The step size was kept as 1 eV.

The blood was collected from the rat and centrifuged at 3000 rpm (4 °C) for 20 min using a centrifuge (REMI, C24 Plus). The collected RBCs were dispersed in normal saline. The doped GQDs were mixed with the RBCs dispersion and incubated at 37 °C for 1 h. Following this, the samples were centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 10 min (4 °C) and the absorbance of supernatant was measured at 540 nm using a UV–visible spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). % Hemolysis was calculated using the following equation [27]:
(2)
% Hemolysis =
A
doped
GQDs
−
A
NC
A
PC
−
A
NC
× 100

A
doped GQDs , A
NC and A
PC represent absorbance of the doped GQDs, negative control (saline) and positive control (20 % Triton X-100), respectively.

The solution of the doped GQDs (0.01 %) was added into the DPPH (0.005 %) solution followed by incubation in dark for 1 h. The absorbance was measured at 515 nm using a UV–visible spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) and the free radical scavenging activity was calculated by following equation [28]:
(3)
% Free
radical
scavenging
activity =
A
0
− A
i
A
0
× 100

A
0 refers to the absorbance of the DPPH solution comprising water and A
i refers to the absorbance of the DPPH solution comprising the doped GQDs.

The doped GQDs and BSA were mixed with phosphate buffer saline (PBS) 7.4 at different ratio and incubated at 37 °C. The samples were taken at predetermined time intervals for 24 h. The samples were subjected to measure fluorescence property and zeta potential using spectrofluorometer and zetasizer, respectively.
The doped GQDs were exposed to different salt concentration denoted as hypotonic, isotonic, and hypertonic (102, 154, and 308 mM) and analysed using spectrofluorometer and zetasizer.
The change in fluorescence property and zeta potential of the doped GQDs in presence of different pH solutions (2, 3, 4, 5.5, 6.8, 7.4, 8, 12) were analysed using spectrofluorometer and zetasizer.
The samples were exposed to different temperature and examined using spectrofluorometer.

The structural integrity of BSA in presence of the doped GQDs was examined using circular dichroism (CD) spectroscopy. The data were collected between 190–280 nm at a scanning speed of 500 nm/min (37 °C) using a CD spectrophotometer (JASCO, J-1500, U.K.).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface morphology using high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM)
ce:section.ce:para: An aqueous dispersion of the N-GQDs was taken on a 400 mesh copper grid, which was dried prior to the analysis. The images were acquired using HR-TEM microscope (Tecnai G2, F30) with an applied accelerating potential of 200 kV.
The data from each study is presented as mean ± standard deviation (n = 3). The statistical significance (p value) was calculated by ANOVA using Microsoft Excel.

It is expected that the number of defect associated with oxidative functional groups would be largely reduced with heteroatom doping in the order of N > S > B as N possesses higher electron-donating capacity as compared to S and B. The proposed reaction scheme for formation of the doped GQDs is shown in Fig. 1. The N-GQDs were obtained from the hydrothermal reaction between citric acid and urea which led to formation of citric acid amides. With further heating, the citric acid amides are self-assembled into graphene lattice due to dehydration and removal of amino groups upon intermolecular interaction between carboxyl, hydroxyl and amino functional groups. The fluorescence property of the N-GQDs is possibly ascribed to the aggregates of oligomers or the condensed citric acid amides [29]. N atoms with atomic size similar to C atoms may replace C atoms and doped into the graphene core. The electron-donating ability of N atoms can increase electron density and remove large amount of oxidative functional groups which may lead to formation of the N-GQDs according to the structure shown in Fig. 1A. In case of the S-GQDs, the mechanism involves dehydration and intermolecular condensation followed by incorporation of S atoms in graphene backbone. The large atomic size of S atoms could generate several defect which may be increased by formation of bond between S and oxygenated functional groups. The surface oxidative functional groups are not largely affected by S doping as much as N doping considering the difference in electronegativity and hence, the amount of surface oxidative functional groups would be higher than that of the N-GQDs (Fig. 1B). B atoms, with electron-withdrawing property, may increase defect and possess higher oxidative functional groups in comparison to the N and S doped GQDs. The doping of B atoms in graphene lattice may disturb the electron distribution to a higher extent than the one could observe in the N and S doped GQDs due to generation of B related defect (Fig. 1C). Moreover, the distinct reaction temperature and time observed are also ascribed to the distinct properties of the heteroatoms. During the reaction, N source acts as a catalyst and facilitates dehydration which reduces the time required to complete the reaction [30]. N doping is easy and fast in comparison to S and B doping due to the atomic size of the N atoms [31]. Also, the formation of the B-GQDs required high temperature to remove large amount of B related defect and obtain the B-GQDs of small size.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Particle size and zeta potential measurement
ce:section.ce:para: The particle size of the N-GQDs, S-GQDs and B-GQDs was found to be 3.32 ± 1.38 nm, 4.86 ± 1.05 nm and 6.13 ± 1.16 nm, respectively (Fig. S1). The particle size of the N-GQDs (Fig. S1A) represents compact structure due to the removal of a large number of defect associated with oxidative functional groups by N atom [26]. The difference in particle size between these doped GQDs is related to a distinct number of defect introduced post doping which can be seen with change in zeta potential. The zeta potential of the N-GQDs, S-GQDs and B-GQDs was observed to be −11.34 ± 1.37, −13.3 ± 1.60 and −15.3 ± 2.06 mV, respectively ( Table 1). The values imply change in number of oxidative functional groups with altered zeta potential. The change in particle size with the S-GQDs (Fig. S1B) is attributed to discrete atomic size and electronegativity of S [32]. The particle size of the B-GQDs (Fig. S1C) is ascribed to attachment of more number of oxidative functional groups due to electron-withdrawing property of B atom [26].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electronic transitions of the doped GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The change in π to π* and n to π* transitions are clearly evident in Fig. 2. The characteristic absorption peaks of the N-GQDs located at 234 nm and 344 nm are due to π to π* and n to π* transition, respectively. The S-GQDs show a peak representing π to π* transition at 206 nm and a peak at 268 nm associated with C-S bonding [33]. The B-GQDs show peaks representing π to π* and n to π* transition at 240 nm and 332 nm, respectively [34]. Based on the change in peak intensity, it can be clearly seen that π to π* transition related to the CC graphene backbone are more prominent in the N-GQDs as compared to the S-GQDs and B-GQDs. This suggests a relatively high sp2 hybridization in the N-GQDs, resulting from electron-donating ability of N atom and removal of a large number of oxidative functional groups [35]. A significant change in electronic transitions (p < 0.05) demonstrates the influence of nature of the heteroatom which includes the ability of the heteroatom to increase electron density on the graphene plane, the number of defect produced and the location of heteroatom doping. Thus, it is expected that the N-GQDs would exhibit higher fluorescence than the S-GQDs and B-GQDs.

The result shows change in the intensity of the blue fluorescence emitted by the doped GQDs ( Fig. 3). The maximum emission (λem) of the N-GQDs, S-GQDs and B-GQDs was observed at 438 nm, 440 nm, and 443 nm, respectively. A significantly increased fluorescence intensity of the N-GQDs (p < 0.05) is ascribed to the reduced number of non-radiative electron-hole pair recombination resulting from the removal of oxidative functional groups [35]. The high QY value of the N-GQDs (Table 1) indicates the possibility of increased sp2 hybridization in comparison to the S-GQDs and B-GQDs. The increased sp2 hybridized aromatic regions result from the conversion of non-radiative –COOH centers into C-N or –CN radiative electron-hole pair recombination [36]. The QY value of the S-GQDs was found to be lower than that of the N-GQDs as S doping produces a small number of defect via SO functional groups [22]. The decreased fluorescence property of the B-GQDs is associated with the electron withdrawing ability of B which produces emissive traps and non-radiative electron-hole pair recombination [37]. The high fluorescence property of the N-GQDs with blue fluorescence can increase their potential in various biomedical applications. The blue fluorescence offers high spatial resolution which increases contrast between the organs treated with the N-GQDs and background. Hence, the clarity of the images can be improved during bioimaging experiments. This unique characteristic can help in live cell imaging, detection of the change in morphology of the cells at different stages of the disease and accumulation of the nanomaterial in different organs of the body [38]. The increased contrast by blue fluorescence of the N-GQDs may also assists in monitoring the accumulation of drug and drug release in different physiological environment [39]. The high fluorescence property would also increase detection sensitivity which is advantageous for the detection of biomarkers present in small concentration [12]. Additionally, the increased contrast by blue fluorescence of the doped GQDs can increase the surgical precision during fluorescence guided surgery in cancer patients as it can clearly distinguish between tumor and healthy cells [40].

Since the observations from the fluorescence study indicate the possibility of altered crystallinity or sp2 hybridization with a change in the type of dopant, the Raman spectra of the doped GQDs were recorded to obtain an insight into change in hybridization. The change in crystallinity of GQDs with change in type of heteroatom dopant was examined through change in the D and G band intensity representing defect and graphitization, respectively. The doped GQDs show D band at 1348.42 cm−1 and G band at 1577.90 cm−1 (Fig. S2). The Raman spectrum of the N-GQDs shows high intensity of the G band in comparison to the D band. The intensity of the G band was decreased with the S-GQDs followed by the B-GQDs. This pattern demonstrates that N doping increases sp2 hybridization or graphitization to a higher extent than the S-GQDs and B-GQDs which is also supported by intensity ratio of D-band to G-band (ID/IG) ratio. The value of ID/IG ratio is 0.12, 0.16 and 0.22 for the N-GQDs, S-GQDs and B-GQDs, respectively (Table 1). The results clearly prove high QY of the N-GQDs. Apart from high QY, the increased graphitization can increase the loading of hydrophobic drug through π-π interaction which can increase the amount of drug at specific site of action.

The composition of the doped GQDs in context with change in the number of oxidative functional groups and aromatic regions was examined with XPS analysis. The presence of the N1s, S2p and B1s peaks confirm the successful doping ( Fig. 4A–C). The fitting curve of the N1s peak showed peaks related to pyrrolic N (399.8 eV) and graphitic N (402.5 eV), which indicates that N is embedded into the graphitic structure [41]. The S2p peak displays peaks at 161.9 and 163.6 eV related to C-S-C and CS bonding, (Fig. 4B) confirming that S atoms are doped in the graphene plane as well as on the surface of GQDs [42]. The B1s peak at 191.2 eV (Fig. 4C) shows that B atoms are doped in BCO2 form [43]. The type of BCO2 doping (–B-CO) increases defect, which explains the increased ID/IG ratio and reduced fluorescence property. The change in number of aromatic regions is also evident through change in intensity of the C1s and O1s peaks of the doped GQDs. The C1s peak at 284.5 eV represents sp2 hybridized regions associated with conjugated C atoms (CC) while the O1s peak at 532.6 eV displays the presence of oxidative functional groups (-COOH). The highly organized conjugated aromatic graphene backbone in the N-GQDs is evident via higher intensity of the C1s peak (Fig. 4D) which is considerably reduced in the S-GQDs (Fig. 4E). This signifies the presence of a lower number of CC groups or aromatic regions in the S-GQDs. A small peak at 283.8 eV in fitting curve corresponds to CS bonding [44]. Moreover, the intensity of the O1s peak is higher than that of the N-GQDs (Fig. 4G and H). This shows that the defect associated with oxidative functional groups are increased upon S doping. With B doping, a significant reduction in the CC structure and amplification in oxidative functional groups are observed through the reduced intensity of the C1s and increased intensity of the O1s peaks (Fig. 4F and I). From these observations, it is confirmed that N atoms are embedded in the form of C-N, pyrrolic N, and graphitic N in GQDs upon N doping. S atoms are bonded with the graphene structure in the form of CS and –C-SO. B atoms are embedded into GQDs via the –B-CO form. These configurations alter the electron density and distribution of the π electron clouds which eventually affect the fluorescence property of the doped GQDs. The doping of N atoms in the form of graphitic N and pyrrolic N offers uniform distribution of delocalised π electrons and removes large amount of surface oxidative functional groups responsible for non-radiative electron-hole pair recombination. The doping of S atoms in graphene lattice and attachment of S atoms with O disturb the distribution of the π electrons and increase the number of defect sites. B was found to be bonded with O in –B-CO form and could not remove large amount of oxidative functional groups which was clearly evident through intensity of the O1s peak. This shows that the configuration of the doped heteroatom also affects the fluorescence property of the doped GQDs [13]. Conclusively, the XPS spectra depict that the N-GQDs have a relatively high number of electronic transition related to conjugated CC structure and the configuration of the doped N atom creates electron rich environment which ultimately offers high QY and makes them a promising nanomaterial for fluorescence based biomedical applications.

The doped GQDs with ultra-small size are likely to damage RBCs to a high extent, given that nanomaterials of small size have a higher potential of damaging RBCs as compared to large-sized nanomaterials [45]. Thus, to ensure safety, the N-GQDs, S-GQDs, and B-GQDs were evaluated for hemolysis. The values (Table 1) meet the ASTM E2524–08 standard, set by the American Society for Testing and Materials. As a result, the N-GQDs, S-GQDs and B-GQDs are regarded as hemocompatible and can be used in live cell imaging, biosensing, drug delivery and photothermal therapy which require direct administration of the nanomaterials.

The heteroatom doping has been reported to have a considerable impact on the antioxidant properties of carbon-based nanomaterials, which ultimately define their significant contribution to free radical neutralization and potential application in the biomedical area [46]. The N-GQDs showed higher antioxidant activity than the S-GQDs and B-GQDs (Table 1). The high antioxidant activity of the N-GQDs is attributed to the increased π electron density within the graphene structure which increases the interaction between the DPPH free radical and the N-GQDs by increasing the transfer of lone pair of electrons [47]. The decreased antioxidant activity of the S-GQDs and B-GQDs exhibits reduced interaction due to decreased π electron clouds. The order in which antioxidant activity is reduced, establishes correlation between the extent of graphitization and free radical scavenging activity. The high free radical scavenging activity of the N-GQDs can increase drug stability when used as a drug carrier.

The colloidal stability in terms of emission is crucial in determination of integrity of the doped GQDs in physiological settings to identify their potential for biomedical applications. With this consideration, the colloidal stability of the doped GQDs was studied in presence of BSA, salt concentration, pH and temperature.

There was no change in λem of the doped GQDs observed upon incubation with BSA over 24 h. This depicts no change in the structure of BSA as well as the fluorescence property of the doped GQDs. Nonetheless, the reduced fluorescence intensity with increased incubation time was noticed owing to the increased hydrophobic interaction between the doped GQDs and BSA (Fig. S3).

As the concentration of BSA was increased, the fluorescence intensity of the N-GQDs was decreased ( Fig. 5A). This is attributed to the adsorption of BSA on the surface of the N-GQDs via π-π interaction. The fluorescence intensity was observed to be increased at N-GQDs:BSA ratio higher than 1:3. It depicts that the surface of the N-GQDs is completely covered by BSA at 1:3 ratio. The further increase in concentration of BSA facilitates desorption of BSA from the surface of the N-GQDs [48]. The zeta potential values verify this observation. The zeta potential of the N-GQDs increased from −11.34 ± 1.37 mV to −1.78 ± 0.86 mV with increased concentration of BSA (1:0–1:3) which was decreased to −11.0 ± 1.56 mV at N-GQDs:BSA ratio of 1:6 (Fig. 5B). The S-GQDs displayed a similar pattern with lower intensity and zeta potential than the N-GQDs owing to the presence of higher number of defect as compared to the N-GQDs. Conversely, maximum interaction between the B-GQDs and BSA was witnessed at B-GQDs:BSA ratio of 1:4. The higher number of oxidative functional groups in the B-GQDs could have provided more surface for adsorption of BSA molecules through strong electrostatic interaction.

The fluorescence intensity of the doped GQDs was observed to be reduced in presence of NaCl solution ( Fig. 6A) owing to electron-withdrawing functional groups produced upon electrostatic interaction between Na+ and –COO- [31]. The chloride ions also decrease the fluorescence intensity via increasing non-radiative electron-hole pair recombination [49]. However, there was no notable variation in the fluorescence intensity of the doped GQDs treated with different NaCl concentration which can also be seen with zeta potential values (Fig. 6B). This indicates good stability of the doped GQDs in various physiological salt solutions.

It is also imperative to monitor the stability of the doped GQDs in the presence of varied pH for various reasons, viz. bioimaging, biosensing and drug delivery monitoring. For such purpose, the fluorescence property of the doped GQDs was examined at different pH: 2, 3, 4, 5.5, 6.8, 7.4, 8, 12. It is clearly evident that the fluorescence intensity of the doped GQDs is reduced with increasing pH (from 2 to 4) owing to change in protonation ( Fig. 7A). The protonation effect is predominant at low pH which reduces number of –COOH and eventually enhances fluorescence property in comparison to the one observed at alkaline pH (8 and 12) [50]. It is also supported by zeta potential values. The zeta potential of the N-GQDs was found to be +2.59 ± 0.56 mV at low pH and shifted to negative side (from −9.43 ± 0.72 mV to −28.5 ± 2.31 mV) at alkaline pH due to increased amount of –COO- moieties (Fig. 7B). There was no considerable change observed in the fluorescence intensity of the doped GQDs at pH 5.5, 6.8, and 7.4. It indicates that no significant change in charge transfer of surface oxidative functional groups (-COOH) occurs at these pH values. This suggests that the doped GQDs can be used as a reliable fluorescent probe for bioimaging and biosensing covering different physiological pH.

The doped GQDs were exposed to varied solution temperature ranging from 2 to 140 °C. The fluorescence intensity of the N-GQDs remained intact up to 80 °C and reduced with further increase in temperature. The fluorescence was completely diminished after 120 °C (Fig. S4). This is ascribed to the formation of large aggregates resulting from increased random motion of electrons at high temperature which eventually reduces the fluorescence [34]. The fluorescence integrity of the N-GQDs up to 80 °C indicates high crystallinity with an abundance of electron-hole pair radiative recombination [34]. Contrary, the S-GQDs were able to maintain fluorescence up to 40 °C owing to a lower extent of crystallinity than the N-GQDs. The B-GQDs, with higher number of defect, exhibited low fluorescence stability at high temperature.

These observations demonstrate the superiority of the N-GQDs in various biomedical applications, which could provide reliable results in fluorescence based imaging and sensing while preventing aggregation in these physiological conditions. The good colloidal stability also assists in drug delivery application such as monitoring of drug release in different pH conditions without hampering the fluorescence property. With the high stability against high temperature, the N-GQDs can be considered as a promising tool for photothermal therapy. Hence, the safety of the N-GQDs also needs to be considered to showcase their potential for biomedical applications which was demonstrated by observing the structural integrity of BSA in the presence of the doped GQDs.

The CD spectra of BSA exposed to the doped GQDs is displayed in Fig. 8. There was no change in the pattern of the α-helical structure of BSA observed upon interaction with the N-GQDs. The reduced intensity is ascribed to the hydrophobic interaction between the N-GQDs and BSA. However, the α-helical structure of BSA was observed to be damaged by the S-GQDs and B-GQDs which is clearly observed through change in pattern [51]. This shows that the N-GQDs can be considered for safe administration over the S-GQDs and B-GQDs.
Hence, the results of all the studies demonstrate that N doping is beneficial in enhancing the potential of GQDs for biomedical applications over S and B doping.

The image of the N-GQDs shows small and uniformly distributed spherical shape particles ( Fig. 9A) with particle size distribution in the range of 1–5 nm (Fig. 9B). The maximum %count of the N-GQDs was found to be 2.71 nm. The nanomaterial of such small size facilitates movement across inter-endothelial gaps and can be used for label-free imaging as well as treatment of cancer cells [52]. The N-GQDs of such small size can easily penetrate blood-brain barrier and thus are considered as a promising drug carrier in treatment of neuronal disorders [53]. Due to the ultra-small size, the N-GQDs can be easily accumulated in higher amount which highlights their application as drug nanocarrier, photosensitizer and antibacterial agent [54,55]. Fig. 9C exhibits clear graphitic lesions with a highly organized hexagonal structure. A selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern depicts bright spots in a symmetric hexagonal pattern (Fig. 9D). It shows that the produced N-GQDs are uniformly grown during synthesis and possess single crystalline nature with a well-arranged hexagonal graphene backbone. It also highlights that the graphene backbone is free of defect known as rotational stacking faults (RSFs) which is responsible for excellent fluorescence stability of the N-GQDs at high temperature [56]. The observations support the result of the Raman study stating that the higher graphitization of the N-GQDs contributes to high fluorescence and antioxidant properties. A fast Fourier transform (FFT) pattern was obtained from the SAED pattern, which is displayed in the inset of Fig. 9D. The image shows a d spacing value, noted as 0.253 ± 0.04 nm, which is considered the distance between two graphitic planes embedded with N atoms.

This study highlights the impact of the type of heteroatom doping (N, S and B) on the properties of GQDs which can bring various benefits to the biomedical field. The doped GQDs were successfully prepared by hydrothermal treatment. With the change in the type of dopant, the difference in structural and chemical properties is evident. The electronic transition related to aromatic regions or sp2 hybridization is observed in the order of N-GQDs>S-GQDs>B-GQDs which eventually increases the fluorescence property. The high QY of the N-GQDs shows their potential for biological structures imaging with increased contrast, detection of a molecule with high sensitivity, real-time monitoring of drug release and distribution within the body. The high free radical neutralization capacity of the N-GQDs could assist in the enhancement of drug delivery efficiency. The doped GQDs are safe for direct administration into the blood stream. Moreover, the doped GQDs are suitable for fluorescence based imaging, sensing and drug delivery monitoring in various physiological settings. The N-GQDs could maintain the fluorescence stability over the wide range of temperature change and did not hamper the structure of BSA, unlike the S-GQDs and B-GQDs. This shows that the N-GQDs could serve as a potential alternative to organic dyes for long-term fluorescence-based bioimaging and biosensing over the S-GQDs and B-GQDs. The N-GQDs are also appropriate for applications involving the use of elevated temperature, such as photothermal therapy and laser tissue welding. Overall, the study suggests that N doping is more beneficial than S and B doping and the N-GQDs can be further utilised for targeted drug delivery and diagnosis.
Mitali Patel: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization. Vrushti Kansara: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing interests.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are grateful for the financial support provided under SHODH (Scheme of Developing High Quality Research) scheme to Vrushti Kansara by the Education Department, Government of Gujarat. We also offer our sincere gratitude to IIT Bombay and IIT Sonipat for providing central laboratory facilities for analysis.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2024.133906.
